interrogator_dossier:
# HEICO Corporation (HEI) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8 · Capital-committee dossier · transcripts FQ2'25 → FQ2'26 (six consecutive calls) cross-referenced against screener financials*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Across six consecutive calls the core story does not shift — it **compounds**. That is itself the signal: a company whose narrative is stable, specific, and serially over-delivered is rare. The arc widens in three deliberate ways.

**(a) From "Wencor integration" to "firing on all engines."** In FQ2'25 (2025-05-28) the marginal story is still the Wencor combination — *"the Wencor and Legacy HEICO operations continue to exceed our expectations… this was an excellent combination"* — and the proof point is 14% organic FSG growth on top of deals. By FQ2'26 (2026-05-28) Wencor is barely mentioned; the company is *"firing on all engines, business is very strong for us virtually across the board"* with FSG organic at **19%** and ETG organic at **17%**. The driver set has broadened from one transformative deal to a fully-firing two-engine machine.

**(b) Defense / space / Golden Dome introduced and amplified.** Defense is a recurring, escalating theme. FQ3'25 frames defense as *"an excellent opportunity, especially as the current administration prioritizes defense and cost efficiency."* By FQ1'26 (2026-02-26) it is explicit and dated to policy: management is asked about the *"$1.5 trillion budget"* and *"Golden Dome,"* and answers candidly — *"there's no Golden Dome department… we're down a layer in the supplier base… both are positives for us."* By FQ2'26 defense is *"now experiencing this in our defense sales, in our defense orders and our defense backlog… a multiyear tail for which we are very well prepared."* This is a genuine secular accelerant being layered onto the aftermarket base, but management is careful never to over-claim a single program.

**(c) M&A cadence is relentless and rising.** Five acquisitions in FY25 (Rosen, Gables — *"third-largest in HEICO's history"* — plus three more), then four more in 1H FY26 (Axillon fuel containment, Sherwood, Ethos, others), all funded by operating cash. The capital-allocation engine is the constant: *"a core tenet… is to fund our organic growth with cash generated by operations and not incurred debt"* (FQ3'25).

**Founder transition is the one genuinely new event.** Larry Mendelson (Chairman/CEO, 36 years) **died** between FQ3'25 and FQ4'25; the FQ4'25 (2025-12-19) and FQ1'26 calls open with extended eulogies, and Victor & Eric are now Co-Chairmen/Co-CEOs. The succession was pre-built (*"that was his succession plan… it worked perfectly"*) and the results did not blink — but a director must weigh that the founder-anchor is gone.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | Financials say | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record results… consolidated net income +49%" | FQ2'26 | FY25 NI $690M vs FY24 $514M (+34%); latest-Q EPS YoY **+48%**; TTM diluted EPS $5.60 | **Corroborated** — the +49% Q is real and the TTM EPS confirms acceleration, not a one-off |
| "Strong organic growth" (13–19% FSG) | all 6 | Revenue +16.3% YoY, **3-yr CAGR +26.6%**; organic is a large share of that | **Corroborated** — organic genuinely double-digit, deals additive on top |
| "Real cash, not artificial GAAP metrics" | FQ4'25/FQ1'26 | FCF +40.3% YoY, 3-yr CAGR +25.5%; **TTM FCF $926M**; FCF margin 19.2% | **Strongly corroborated** — cash conversion is the spine of the model and the numbers back the rhetoric |
| "We delever quickly… funded by operations" | FQ3'25 | Net debt/EBITDA fell 2.06x → 1.60x (Oct-25) even after $630M of deals; recomputed **net debt $2.38B** | **Corroborated with nuance** — leverage is modest and self-amortizing, but this is NET DEBT, not net cash; the balance sheet is clean, not pristine |
| "Margin expansion… we're busting at the seams" | FQ2'26 | Net margin 15.4%, FCF margin 19.2%; gross margin 39.8% flagged **contracting**; margin **1.45σ above historical peak** | **Mixed** — operating leverage is real, but the screener flags gross margin contraction and margins sitting 1.45σ rich; the upside is being earned at a cyclical high-water mark |
| Beat cadence ("record" every quarter) | all 6 | **Beat 7/7 (100%)**; EPS +33.5% YoY, EPS 5yr CAGR +22% | **Corroborated** — flawless recent delivery |
| ROIC / returns quality | — | **ROIC 10.0%, ROE 14.0%** | **Tension** — for a "compounder" the reported ROIC is only ~10%, depressed by acquisition goodwill/intangibles; the *incremental* economics are far better than the blended return suggests, but the headline return is unremarkable for the multiple paid |

**Where the story is richer than the numbers:** the "compounder at any scale" framing implies a premium return profile, yet blended ROIC is ~10% — the multiple (P/FCF ~55x) is paying for *durability and reinvestment runway*, not for an exceptional spot return. **Where it is poorer than the trajectory justifies:** nowhere material — this is the unusual case where the financials are at least as strong as the rhetoric. The forensic risk is not credibility; it is **price**.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Rising / Stable-high.** Confidence is consistently high and, if anything, climbs into FQ2'26.

- FQ2'25: *"filled with deep optimism… uniquely positioned"* — Eric: *"our team members literally hit the ball out of the park."*
- FQ3'25: *"robust double-digit organic growth… we remain very optimistic."*
- FQ4'25: through Larry's death — *"more optimistic than ever about this company's prospects."*
- FQ1'26: a notably *philosophical* call — Eric volunteers the "secret sauce" monologue (*"obedience to the unenforceable became our DNA"*), a confident, almost legacy-defining tone.
- FQ2'26: peak confidence — *"firing on all engines… HEICO has always been and will always be where the industry goes and where it grows."*

**No defensive pivot.** The language stays **offensive** (growth, investment, market-share gains) throughout. The only defensive notes are honest, unsolicited macro caveats — the China tariff pause (FQ2'25), the *"current just war"* / Middle East softness and a ~$15–20M defense pull-forward (FQ2'26). Management *volunteers* the negatives rather than burying them, which raises rather than lowers credibility. Guidance specificity actually **increases** late: Carlos gives a concrete FSG margin range (*"24% to 26%"*) and ETG range in FQ2'26 — more numeric guidance than earlier calls.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH.** HEICO is famously light on formal numeric guidance (no annual EPS guide), so the test is *qualitative promises kept*. On that basis the record is excellent:

- "Accretive within the first/year following acquisition" (Rosen, Gables) — pattern historically delivered; deals visibly additive to segment records.
- Carlos's FQ3'25 hedge that Gables amortization *"will dampen the OI margin a little"* but *"not as pronounced as Exxelia"* — followed by FQ2'26 record ETG margins (+56% operating income), i.e. the conservative framing under-promised.
- The LCP-distribution disclosure (FQ1'26): management flagged a ~$73M operating-cash drag for the remainder of FY26 but explained it is *"net cash neutral"* (funded by corporate-owned life insurance, an investing inflow) — proactive, granular, non-promotional disclosure of a metric-distorting item.

**Sandbagging pattern:** mild and structural — they consistently frame margin/accretion conservatively, then beat. Combined with **7/7 beats** in the financials, this is a sandbag-and-beat profile, not a stretch-and-miss one. **Score: HIGH.**

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Analysts probe four recurring areas; management answers most directly, deflects two for competitive reasons:

1. **Centralizing the supply chain for margin** (Liwag, FQ3'25): pushed on how much margin a centralized purchasing function could capture. Carlos **declines to quantify** (*"I haven't done a study to give you a precise number"*) and argues the decentralized model is worth the cost. Honest, but a recurring un-modeled margin lever left vague.
- **2. PMA pricing power / contract roll-offs** (Liwag, FQ3'25): analysts see latent pricing (parts at 50–70% OEM discount); Eric explicitly **refuses the profit-grab** (*"that's really not the HEICO way… I would not model that kind of activity"*). A deliberate decision to leave near-term margin on the table — bullish for durability, a drag on the bear's "margins are peaking" thesis.
3. **Specific engine/PMA program penetration & defense end-market splits** (Khanna, Raffetto): repeatedly **deflected** *"for competitive reasons"* / *"we did not break that out publicly."* Standard for HEICO; not evasive about results, only about competitive granularity.
4. **The dual-class A/common structure** (Khanna, FQ4'25): Victor calls Class A *"a screaming value"* that *"a rational market will recognize"* — a soft governance topic management treats as status-quo.

No instance in the window where a deflected topic later became bad news. Boeing MAX destocking (Ciarmoli, FQ3'25) was flagged as a risk and management's *"little bit"* answer has so far proven accurate.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Valuation, not operations.** Every methodological margin-of-safety is deeply negative (DCF −100%, EPV −100%, OwnerErn −100%, IV15 −100%, Graham −58%); P/FCF **54.8x**, P/E ~62x. The market has fully priced the quality. *(Screener; corroborated by external P/E ~61.6x vs industry ~40x.)*
🔴 **Margins 1.45σ above historical peak** and **gross margin flagged contracting** — incremental upside is being booked at a cyclical/structural high.
🔴 **Insider open-market signal is negative:** 21 buys (17,613 sh) vs 13 sells (**230,496 sh**) — sells dwarf buys ~13:1 by share count. Family/insiders are net sellers into strength.
🔴 **Founder-anchor gone** (Larry Mendelson died, late 2025) — succession pre-built, but the 36-year culture-keeper is no longer a backstop.
🔴 **ROIC ~10% / Piotroski 5/9** — blended returns and the F-score are merely average, diluted by acquisition intangibles.

🟢 **Financials corroborate the narrative on every major axis** — organic growth, FCF conversion, beat cadence, self-funded deleveraging. Rare alignment.
🟢 **FAA-PMA + DER repair franchise** is a genuine regulatory/intangible moat with switching costs (qualified parts on flight-critical platforms).
🟢 **Multi-engine secular tailwind** — aftermarket supercycle + defense/space budget expansion + Golden Dome layer, with HEICO's low-cost PMA positioned exactly where budget-cost-pressure points.
🟢 **Honest, proactive disclosure** — volunteers macro softness, the LCP cash-flow distortion, and refuses promotional moves (no profit-grab, no multiple-chasing).

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology shift toward permanence.** Early calls sell a *quarter*; FQ1'26–FQ2'26 sell an *institution* ("do the right thing," "obedience to the unenforceable," "where the industry goes and where it grows"). Post-founder-death, management is consciously codifying the culture as the durable asset — a tell that the *people* risk is top-of-mind even as results stay clean.
- **The "no one-time charges in 36 years" boast** (FQ1'26) is a pointed, unprompted swipe at peers who *"erase upcycle earnings after a black swan."* It is a credibility flex aimed squarely at the bear case that aerospace earnings are cyclical — and it is backed by the clean trajectory.
- **Class A "screaming value"** language (FQ4'25) is a quiet signal that *even management thinks part of its own equity is mispriced* — but that is the low-vote A shares vs the common, an intra-structure arb, not a statement that the enterprise multiple is cheap.
- **CapEx held at ~1.5–1.6% of revenue** through a record-demand surge — asset-light, cash-generative model intact; they are not chasing growth with capital intensity.
- **Defense framing as "speed, not cost-plus"** (FQ4'25): Victor positions HEICO as structurally advantaged for a DOGE-style cost/speed defense regime — a subtle re-rating argument that the defense mix is *higher-quality* than cost-plus primes.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STRENGTHENING.** The earnings base is widening (two engines firing, defense backlog building, M&A compounding), cash conversion is excellent, leverage is falling, and the narrative is corroborated by the financials on every material axis. This is an operationally pristine compounder accelerating into a genuine secular tailwind.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** this is a **price problem, not a quality problem.** At P/FCF ~55x / P/E ~62x with *every* intrinsic-value method showing a deeply negative margin of safety, the stock already capitalizes flawless multi-year execution. The forensic edge here is **negative** — there is no expectations gap to arbitrage; consensus and the tape already love it (stock +7% vs 200d, 75% up the 52-wk range, analyst targets being chased upward to $390–410). The "catalyst" (defense/aftermarket supercycle) is already firing in the price, not a hidden re-rate. Insiders are net sellers into the strength. Size for a *quality-compounder-at-a-rich-multiple* (a hold/own-the-business decision), **not** for a mispriced special-situation re-rate.

**Economic moat:** intangible-asset + regulatory-license (FAA-PMA approvals, DER repair authority) layered with switching-costs (qualified parts on flight-critical, long-life platforms). Judged on the EVIDENCE: gross margin is **contracting** (39.8%, flagged) and blended **ROIC is ~10%** (diluted by acquisition intangibles) — so the *durable, high-return aftermarket core* is wide, but the *blended* return profile is being held flat-to-down by the acquisitive structure and a margin sitting 1.45σ above its own peak. Net read: **WIDE moat, STABLE trend** — the franchise is durable and the secular tailwind is real, but it is not visibly *widening* on the reported returns; the premium is for durability, not for an accelerating return on capital.

**Secular force / terminal value:** No structural terminal threat. The aftermarket-PMA model is *anti-fragile* to the obvious tails — higher OEM prices and budget cost-pressure make HEICO's discount parts *more* attractive (Eric, FQ2'26: fuel-price spikes are bringing *new* PMA customers). The risks are **cyclical** (a commercial-aerospace/air-traffic downturn, a defense-budget reversal), not secular impairment of the franchise. Cheapness is irrelevant here because the stock is **not cheap** — this is a quality name at a fear-free, fully-priced multiple, the inverse of a value trap and the inverse of a fear-print.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 5 | TRAJECTORY: STRENGTHENING | MOAT: WIDE | MOAT_TREND: STABLE | SECULAR_THREAT: none

bull_thesis: HEICO is a best-in-class quality compounder firing on every cylinder, and the most recent quarter (Q2 of fiscal 2026, reported 2026-05-27) confirms the engine is speeding up rather than maturing. Earnings came in at $1.66 per share against the $1.33 analysts expected, a 25% beat and the eighth straight quarter of beating estimates. Net income rose 49%, sales rose 25%, and the Flight Support Group (the aftermarket parts and repair business) grew 19% on an organic basis, meaning growth from the existing business rather than from acquisitions, with its operating margin widening to 26.2% from 24.1%; the Electronic Technologies Group reached a 26.5% margin. The case rests on three durable pillars. First, a wide and still-widening competitive advantage rooted in FAA Parts Manufacturer Approval, the regulatory license that takes years to earn; once HEICO's parts are certified onto a fleet at a 30-70% discount to the original equipment maker's price, it is costly for an airline to switch away and demand sticks for the decades-long life of the aircraft, and the Wencor combination (which management called 'the gift that keeps on giving' in the Q2 fiscal-2025 call) shifts the mix toward higher-margin approved parts and mechanically raises profitability. Second, a self-funding acquisition machine that has never broken stride: five deals in fiscal 2025 plus Ethos, Axillon, Sherwood, and Southwest Antennas heading into fiscal 2026, all paid for out of operating cash, with net debt actually falling to 1.74 times annual cash earnings (EBITDA, or earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization) even after a heavy year of deals; the Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 8.4 signals a very safe balance sheet. Third, long-running tailwinds in the aftermarket and in defense, including aging fleets, shortages of original-maker parts that force airlines toward HEICO's approved alternatives, and large defense programs such as Golden Dome against a $1.5 trillion budget across both segments. A conservative accounting culture, including deliberate reserving and the fact that the company has 'never had a one-time unusual charge,' with operating cash flow running at 130% of net income, means the reported earnings likely understate the true economics. Revenue has compounded at 26.6% a year over three years, earnings per share at 24.3%, and trailing-twelve-month free cash flow stands at $926 million. The bull's bet is simple: pay a premium for a proven grower of 20%-plus with no structural threat and let it grow into the price. Analysts uniformly raised their targets to an average near $386 after the quarter.
bear_thesis: This is not a bad business; it is a fully priced one. HEI trades at roughly 60 times trailing earnings and about 63 times forward earnings, and 55 times free cash flow: a premium of about 60% to its closest premium-aftermarket peer TransDigm (around 37-38 times earnings, verified in June 2026) and about 22% above HEICO's own ten-year average of roughly 51 times earnings. Every method of estimating intrinsic value comes out 30% to 100% too rich. A discounted-cash-flow estimate (projecting future cash flows back to today's value) leaves no margin of safety at all, and the same is true for earnings-power value, owner-earnings, and a Graham-style estimate, which sits 59% below the price; the price is also 7% above the screener's Buffett-style fair value. Analysts expect earnings to grow only 12.8% next year, so paying 60 times earnings for a low-double-digit grower implies a price-to-growth ratio near 5 with no cushion. The event the bull case leans on, more blowout momentum, has already happened with the Q2 fiscal-2026 report: the stock surged and analysts uniformly raised targets, so the re-rating is already spent. There is no special-situation edge here, no spinoff, no activist investor, no fixed binding event, and no asset sale. Insiders' open-market activity tilts toward selling: 21 buys totaling 17,613 shares against 13 sells totaling 230,496 shares by volume. Return on invested capital is 10% and return on equity 14%, respectable but well short of the 30%-plus that a 60-times multiple usually implies; it is the multiple, not the underlying returns, doing the work. Founder and capital-allocator Larry Mendelson has just died (Q4 fiscal-2025). The bear's trigger is any decelerating-organic-growth quarter, a richly priced acquisition that lifts net debt above about 2.5 times earnings, a softening aerospace cycle (Boeing MAX destocking was flagged in the Q3 fiscal-2025 call), or a broad re-rating lower of expensive quality names; any of those would hit a 60-times stock hard, with no valuation floor to catch it.
consensus_delta: There is no favorable gap between our view and the market's here; if anything the market is positioned alongside the quality, not against it. Consensus from 20 analysts is a Buy with an average target near $386 (Goldman at $471, Jefferies at $410), already extrapolating the compounding, and targets were uniformly raised after the 25% earnings beat in Q2 fiscal-2026. The street is not looking the wrong way on the business. The only real gap is on valuation discipline: the market is implicitly underwriting sustained 20%-plus compounding to justify 60 times earnings, while the forward consensus calls for just 12.8% earnings growth, and that gap resolves against the bull, because a low-double-digit grower does not support a 60-times multiple (a price-to-growth ratio near 5). The screener's outdated flag that gross margin is contracting is wrong, since margins are in fact expanding, so the bear cannot even lean on slowing operations yet; the bear case is purely about the price.
forcing_function: There is no catalyst here in the special-situation sense, meaning nothing that forces the market to re-rate the stock. There is no proxy-vote window, deal vote, spinoff record date, divestiture deadline, or activist investor. The only scheduled events are routine quarterly results (the next is roughly 65 days out) and twice-yearly dividend declarations, neither of which forces a re-rating; they are momentum check-ins, not consensus-shattering events. The catalyst the bull case relied on, the blowout Q2 fiscal-2026 quarter, already fired on 2026-05-27 and is in the price. With no fixed binding event and no asymmetry, the name does not clear the bar for catalyst density.
valley_of_death: There is no balance-sheet danger zone, no risky window in the months just ahead driven by the books: net debt is 1.74 times annual cash earnings, the Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score is 8.4, operating cash flow runs at 130% of net income, and there is no wall of maturing debt and no cash-burn hump. The near-term risk is purely a re-rating lower, not a solvency problem. The triggers over the next 3-9 months: first, the next quarter (about 65 days out) laps the toughest year-ago comparisons in company history, so any organic-growth figure below double digits would re-rate a 60-times stock hard; second, fiscal-2026 stock compensation is front-loaded (per CFO Carlos Macau in the Q1 fiscal-2026 call) because of performance-vesting, optically pressuring first-half earnings; third, a large or richly priced acquisition that lifts leverage; fourth, Boeing MAX original-equipment destocking (flagged in the Q3 fiscal-2025 call) bleeding into specialty products; and fifth, a broad compression of multiples across expensive quality and momentum names in a stretched, fully invested market (the Bank of America fund-manager survey shows cash at 3.9%, a sell signal). None of these is dated or binding, but all of them hit hardest at a price that carries no margin of safety.
positioning_washout: There is no forced-selling setup to exploit here. HEICO is not a yield stock owned mainly by income or index funds that would be pushed to sell; it pays only a token semiannual dividend of $0.12 a share, so there is no dividend-cut that would rotate holders out. The shareholder base is long-term quality and growth investors plus index funds, and the dual-class share structure (with Class A called a 'screaming value' by Victor Mendelson in the Q4 fiscal-2025 call) is a static curiosity with no announced trigger to collapse the two classes together. There is no positioning flush to wait for and no forced-selling lever to harvest, and the absence of a wash-out is itself a reason there is no entry edge.
moderator_conclusion: Our recommendation is to pass for now, with low conviction (a 2 on our 1-to-5 scale). HEICO is an A-plus business at a D-minus price, and our market framework specifically penalizes exactly this profile. The forensic read is pristine: top credibility, an improving trajectory, a wide and widening competitive advantage from its FAA parts-approval franchise, accelerating organic growth of 19% in the Flight Support Group, expanding margins, falling debt, eight straight earnings beats, and no structural threat. But the entire question for us is valuation, and the answer is unambiguous: about 60 times trailing earnings and 63 times forward earnings, 55 times free cash flow, no margin of safety on any intrinsic-value model, a price 7% above its own Buffett-style fair value, a premium of about 60% to TransDigm and about 22% above its own ten-year average, and a base-case sum-of-the-parts value (valuing each business line separately) of about $210, some 35-40% below the $335 price, with even a generous bull sum-of-the-parts of about $277 failing to reach today's price. The catalyst the case leaned on, continued blowout momentum, fired with the Q2 fiscal-2026 quarter and is fully reflected; analysts uniformly raised targets, so the re-rating is spent. Applying our market framework: the tape is stretched and bullish (the fund-manager survey shows cash at 3.9%, a sell signal, and the largest technology names and quality names are crowded), and the approach tilts hard toward lightly-covered structural special situations (distressed names ahead of spinoffs ahead of forced-seller setups) while deprioritizing already-fired, momentum-driven catalysts in heavily-covered, top-priced quality names, which is precisely HEICO. The framework moves this down, not up. There is no special-situation asymmetry, no catalyst that forces a re-rating, no forced-selling wash-out, and no margin of safety. Judged purely on value, with the framework set aside and the catalyst ignored, conviction is also a 2: the moat is wide and widening so no value-trap penalty applies, and the forensic quality keeps the score above 1, but a superb compounder with negative upside even to its own sum-of-the-parts and roughly 40-55% downside to a normal multiple is a low-conviction name on price alone. If HEI ever falls toward the high-$100s or low-$200s (its parts value), it becomes a genuine 4-to-5; at $335 it is one to watch fall, not to buy.

# HEICO Corporation (HEI) — Deep Dive on Management and the Setup

*A report to the investment committee, drawing on the company's earnings calls from Q2 fiscal-2025 (2025-05-28) through Q1 fiscal-2026 (2026-02-26), four consecutive calls, cross-referenced against the screener's financial trajectory and the live Q2 fiscal-2026 results reported 2026-05-27.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The HEICO story across these four calls is striking for what it does **not** do: it does not evolve, pivot, or reach for a new growth driver. The same engine is recited quarter after quarter with metronomic consistency, namely organic double-digit growth in the Flight Support Group (the aftermarket parts and repair business), bolt-on acquisitions funded entirely by operating cash, and disciplined debt paydown between deals. In Q2 fiscal-2025 Victor Mendelson framed it as "strong 14% organic growth from increased demand across all of our product lines and the impact from our profitable fiscal 2025 and 2024 acquisitions." By Q4 fiscal-2025 the identical sentence structure appeared with "strong 16% organic growth," and by Q1 fiscal-2026 "strong organic growth of 12%." The narrative is a **loop, not an arc**, and for a 36-year compounder that is the point. Management explicitly resists expanding the thesis: in Q3 fiscal-2025 Eric Mendelson rejected centralizing the supply chain for margin ("I don't think culturally... in the next 3 to 5 years"), and in Q1 fiscal-2026 he disavowed price hikes on mature approved parts ("that's really not the HEICO way... I would not model that kind of activity"). The only genuinely new element is the **founder succession**: Larry Mendelson's death surfaced in the Q4 fiscal-2025 call (2025-12-19), handled as a values-transmission story ("That was his succession plan... It worked perfectly") rather than a break. Growth drivers introduced and sustained: defense and space (Golden Dome, a "$1.5T budget"), Wencor cross-selling, and a relentless acquisition cadence (five deals in fiscal 2025, Gables the third-largest in company history, plus Ethos, Axillon, Sherwood, and Southwest Antennas into fiscal 2026).

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS

| Management claim | Where stated | Financial evidence | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record" sales and operating income every single quarter | All 4 calls | Revenue $1.87B to $2.21B to $2.97B to $3.86B to $4.49B (fiscal 2021 through 2025); three-year growth rate +26.6% a year | **Verified** — the records are real, not framing |
| Double-digit organic growth in the Flight Support Group | 14% / 13% / 16% / 12% across calls | Live Q2 fiscal-2026: Flight Support Group up 19% organically, record sales of $929.4M | **Verified and accelerating** |
| Margin leverage from cost discipline | Q3 fiscal-2025 ("revenues outpace SG&A") | Live Q2 fiscal-2026: Flight Support Group operating margin 26.2% (from 24.1%), Electronic Technologies Group 26.5% | **Verified** — the screener's "gross margin contracting" flag is out of date; companywide margins are expanding |
| Cash funds growth, debt stays low | Every call ("never incurred debt to grow organically") | Net debt to cash earnings 1.86x to 1.90x to 1.60x to 1.79x to **1.74x** (Q2 fiscal-2026); debt fell even after $630M of fiscal-2025 deals | **Verified** — disciplined and self-funding |
| Earnings per share compounding | "record net income" repeated | EPS $2.21 to $2.55 to $2.91 to $3.67 to $4.90; trailing diluted $5.60; Q2 fiscal-2026 $1.66 vs $1.33 expected (+25% beat) | **Verified** — beat 7 of 7 quarters, now 8 of 8 |
| "Earnings are real / never a one-time charge" | Q1 fiscal-2026 ("never had a one-time unusual charge") | Free-cash-flow margin 19.2%; trailing free cash flow $926M; operating cash flow 130% of net income (Q3 fiscal-2025) | **Verified** — cash conversion is genuine |

The narrative is **more conservative than the numbers in only one respect**: management is conservative to a fault, repeatedly declining available margin upside (resetting approved-part prices, centralizing the supply chain). The story is, if anything, less aggressive than the trajectory would justify, the opposite of the usual red flag. The one place the valuation diverges from the story: return on invested capital reads 10.0% in the screener, which understates true returns (depressed by acquisition-goodwill accounting on Wencor and Gables) but is not the 30%-plus that a top compounder earns on a clean basis; the multiple (60 times earnings) is doing heavy lifting that the reported returns alone do not support.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: rising, from an already-high base.** Confidence is uniformly, almost euphorically high and rises into fiscal 2026. Q2 fiscal-2025: Eric Mendelson — "Our results were absolutely phenomenal... hit the ball out of the park." Q4 fiscal-2025: "Strong would be an understatement, but 16% organic growth." Q1 fiscal-2026: "We remain very bullish and optimistic." There is **no shift from offensive to defensive language**; the entire vocabulary is growth, investment, and acquisition, never cost-cutting or macro-defense. Tariff and China-destocking questions (Q2 fiscal-2025, Q4 fiscal-2025) were answered with confident dismissal ("we see strength in all of our areas... I wouldn't say that we've seen that"). Guidance specificity is deliberately low, since HEICO famously does not give numeric guidance, but the qualitative confidence is maximal and consistent. The one somber note, Larry Mendelson's passing, was handled as continuity, not vulnerability.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: high.** HEICO provides no formal numeric guidance, so the test is whether the qualitative promises (continued double-digit organic growth, accretive acquisitions, debt paydown) are delivered. They are, every quarter: the screener shows the company beat estimates in 7 of 7 quarters (100%), now 8 of 8 after Q2 fiscal-2026's $1.66 versus $1.33 (+25%). There is a structural pattern of under-promising baked into the culture; Q1 fiscal-2026's discussion of "properly reserving for obsolete inventory... not swept under the rug to protect reported earnings" and "never had a one-time unusual charge" is a deliberate signal that reported earnings are understated relative to economic reality, not inflated. Acquisitions promised "accretive within the year" (Gables, Rosen, and the Exxelia comparables) track to that. The credibility gap is the inverse of most names: the risk is not that management over-promises, but that the market has fully priced the reliability into a 60-times multiple.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Analysts probe four recurring areas, and management's answers hold up. **(1) Tariff and China demand** (Gavin Parsons, UBS, Q2 fiscal-2025; Gautam Khanna, Q4 fiscal-2025) — management acknowledged a brief China order-pause "to send a message" but reported no durable hit; the live Q2 fiscal-2026 print confirms no demand crack. **(2) Margin upside from approved-part pricing** (Kristine Liwag, Morgan Stanley, Q3 fiscal-2025) — management explicitly refused to harvest it, a governance positive but a near-term margin ceiling the bulls cannot model. **(3) The dual-class structure and Class A discount** (Khanna, Q4 fiscal-2025) — Victor called Class A "a screaming value" but signaled "status quo," with no catalyst. **(4) Stock compensation and amortization drag** (Louis Raffetto, Wolfe, Q3 fiscal-2025 and Q1 fiscal-2026) — CFO Carlos Macau flagged elevated stock compensation front-loaded in fiscal 2026 from performance-vesting, tapering later, a modeling nuisance, not a thesis-breaker. **No deflected question later turned into bad news** across this window, a clean record.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

Green flag: **8 of 8 earnings beats and organic growth in the Flight Support Group accelerating to 19% (Q2 fiscal-2026)** — the engine is intact and speeding up.
Green flag: **Margins expanding** (Flight Support Group 24.1% to 26.2%, Electronic Technologies Group to 26.5%), refuting the screener's stale "gross margin contracting" flag.
Green flag: **Self-funding acquisitions and debt falling to 1.74 times cash earnings** even after a heavy deal year, with a pristine balance sheet and an Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 8.4.
Green flag: **Conservative accounting culture** (reserving discipline, no one-time charges, declining easy margin grabs), the rarest green flag of all: management understating economic earnings.
Red flag: **Valuation, not operations, is the concern** — about 60 times trailing and 63 times forward earnings, 55 times free cash flow, and no margin of safety on any model (discounted cash flow, earnings-power value, and owner-earnings all fully rich; the Graham estimate 59% below price); the price is 7% above the screener's own Buffett-style fair value.
Red flag: **Insider open-market activity tilts toward selling** — 21 buys (17,613 shares) versus 13 sells (230,496 shares); the real-conviction signal is net selling by volume, not the grant-inflated buy ratio of 0.08.
Red flag: **Founder-era succession just completed** (Larry Mendelson's death, Q4 fiscal-2025) — execution risk is low given the embedded culture, but the irreplaceable capital-allocator is gone.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

The most revealing subtext is the **deliberate refusal to optimize for the multiple**. Eric Mendelson, Q1 fiscal-2026: "We are not into programs of the year, buzzwords or comparing ourselves to others hoping to get a higher multiple on our shares." This is a tell that management knows the stock is richly valued and is signaling it will not financial-engineer to defend it, a long-term-owner posture that protects long-run value but offers no near-term lever to push the price up. A second signal: the **Class A "screaming value"** remark (Q4 fiscal-2025) plus directors buying Class A, a quiet acknowledgment that the voting-share premium is the only obvious value inside the capital structure, with no plan to close it. A third: **capital spending holding at about 1.5-1.6% of revenue** (Carlos Macau, Q4 fiscal-2025), an asset-light, high-return model, but also confirmation that growth is being bought (through acquisitions) rather than built, so the compounding depends on an unbroken pipeline of deals at sane prices.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Trajectory: turning stronger.** Every operating and balance-sheet measure is improving — accelerating organic growth, expanding margins, falling debt, a healthy acquisition pipeline, and an 8-of-8 beat record. This is a genuine best-in-class quality compounder.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** this is a price problem, not a business problem. The business deserves a premium; the question is whether **60 times trailing earnings on a high-single to low-double-digit forward growth consensus (+12.8%)** leaves any margin of safety, with every intrinsic-value model showing the stock 30-100% above fair value and the catalyst the case leaned on (the Q2 fiscal-2026 blowout) already fired and already in the price (the stock surged on the print and analysts uniformly raised targets to an average near $386). There is no special-situation asymmetry here, no spinoff, no activist, no fixed binding event. The only path to a gain is paying about 60 times and trusting the compounder to grow into it, which is a quality-momentum bet, not a mispricing.

**ECONOMIC MOAT.** Type: a regulatory license plus switching costs plus an intangible franchise. FAA Parts Manufacturer Approval is a years-long, capital- and relationship-intensive certification advantage; airlines that qualify HEICO parts onto fleets face high costs to re-qualify away; and the 30-70% discount to the original maker's price creates demand stickiness. Width: wide. Trend: widening — the evidence is unambiguous: expanding operating margins (Flight Support Group to 26.2%, Electronic Technologies Group to 26.5%), accelerating organic growth (to 19% in the Flight Support Group), Wencor cross-selling shifting the mix toward higher-margin approved parts and raising profitability, and rising earnings and cash-flow growth rates. The reported return on invested capital of 10% understates the true economics (weighed down by acquisition goodwill); on an organic, incremental basis returns are far higher and rising.

**SECULAR THREAT: none.** No structural force impairs HEICO's long-run value. The opposite is true — long-running aftermarket tailwinds (aging fleets, shortages of original-maker parts, airlines seeking cost-effective approved alternatives, defense and space budget expansion). Artificial intelligence, fintech, autonomy, and channel-shift are irrelevant to FAA-approved physical aircraft parts. There is no cheapness signal here: this is not a fear-driven sell-off (the stock is expensive) and not a value trap (the base is growing); it is a fully priced quality compounder. The risk is multiple compression, not the erosion of long-run value.
